fact_texts: The cazique invited the expedition to his village, promising plenty of food and the ability to go on shore in his twelve canoes.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo was a devout Catholic, not superstitious, considered honest, with an unblemished reputation.

Bernal Diaz del Castillo, born in Medina del Campo, León, was a hidalgo of a Christian family, left Castile for the New World in 1514, arrived in Mexico in 1519, fought 119 battles, completed his memoirs on 26 February 1568 in Guatimala at about age 70-86, and died possibly at 86.
The expedition encountered Indians wearing cotton mantels, appeared friendly, and were identified as caziques.
The expedition met priests called papas, who used copal incense and performed rituals, warning them to leave before bundles of reeds were burned.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo claimed that his memoir is the only reliable account of the conquest of New Spain on which we can place reliance.
Priest Gonzalez ordered the expedition to remove gold and small idols to their ships, using two Indians from Cuba.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo completed his memoir on 26 February 1568 in Guatimala, the seat of the royal court of audience, at about age 70, possibly 86, and wrote it in Guatimala.

Don Hernando Cortes, marquis del Valle Oaxaca, claims to have written an account of his travels, completed on 26 February 1568 in Guatimala, addressed to Don Carlos the Fifth and viceroy Don Antonio de Mendoza, covering his departure from Castile, discovery of Yucatan, battles, and interactions with natives
The expedition captured two natives, named Melchior and Julian, who were tattooed around the eyes and later baptized as Christians.
The cazique invited the expedition to his village, promising plenty of food and the ability to go on shore in his twelve canoes.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo claimed that his memoir is the only reliable account of the conquest of New Spain on which we can place reliance.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo completed his memoir on 26 February 1568 in Guatimala, the seat of the royal court of audience, at about age 70, possibly 86, and wrote it in Guatimala.

The memoir 'The Memoirs of the Conquistador Bernal Diaz del Castillo' was written by Bernal Diaz del Castillo, translated from Spanish by John Ingram Lockhart, F.R.A.S., and published by J. Hatchard and Son, 187 Piccadilly, London, MDCCCXLIV, with printers C. and J. Adlard, Batholomew Close.
